coredata.dc:title: In situ construction of N-doped hollow carbon nanotubes anchored Co nanoparticles for bifunctional ORR/OER electrocatalyst
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Zhiwei
Xiong, Qianqian
Fu, Rao
Wang, Wenli
Zhang, Lan
Yan, Minglei
Wu, Chun
Sun, Mengmeng
Su, Gehong
Wang, Yanying
Ye, Jianshan
Rao, Hanbing
coredata.dc:description: The development of efficient, inexpensive, and stable bifunctional non-noble metal catalysts for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is of practical significance for the large-scale application of zinc-air batteries. Here, a novel bifunctional Co-based nano-catalyst (Co@CxNyC) is fabricated by complexation-reduction and high-temperature pyrolysis method. Co nanoparticles were loaded in nitrogen-doped hollow irregular carbon nanotubes derived from natural fibers and carbon quantum dots. Remarkably, the unique hollow structure and the Co-Nx active site make the screened Co@C1N3C have efficient ORR&OER catalytic performance. Co@C1N3C with onset potential (0.90 V) and half-wave potential (0.84 V) exhibited an efficient 4e− transfer path and favorable stability and methanol tolerance. Moreover, the overpotential of Co@C1N3C needed to achieve 100 mA cm−2 is 625 mV, which is 31 mV better than that of RuO2. In conclusion, this work not only provides deep insights into the catalytic activity of Co@CxNyC nanocomposite as ORR&OER bifunctional electrocatalysts but also guides the growth of Co-based bifunctional catalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hollow carbon nanotube
Co NPs
Oxygen reduction reaction
Oxygen evolution reaction
Bifunctional catalyst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 22104102 , 32271352 , and 32371522 ), Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province (Grant Nos. 2023NSFSC0618 , 2022NSFSC1266 , and 2022NSFSC1924 ), Key Laboratory of Fuel Cell Technology of Guangdong Province ( FC202205 ), and the Two-Way Support Team Programs of Sichuan Agricultural University (Grant No. P202105 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) SEM images of (a) Co@C1C, (b) Co@C1N3, and (c) Co@C1N3C. (d) TEM image of Co@C1N3C. (e) HRTEM images of Co@C1N3C. (f) SAED pattern of Co@C1N3C. (g-k) EDX-STEM element mapping images of Co@C1N3C.
(a) Pore size distributions and nitrogen adsorption−desorption isotherms of Co@C1N3C. (b) XRD pattern of Co@C1N3C. (c-f) Full XPS spectrum and plots of C1s, Co 2p, and N 1s of Co@C1N3C.

(a) CVs of Co@C1N3C and Pt/C in 0.1 M KOH saturated N2 or O2. (b) LSVs for various samples at 1600 rpm. (c) LSVs of Co@C1N3C with rotating speed at 225–2025 rpm. (d) Corresponding K-L plots ranging at various potentials. (e) Methanol resistance of Co@C1N3C. (f) Timing current curve of Co@C1N3C in 36000s.

(a) LSV curves and (b) Tafel curves of Co@CxNyC obtained in 1 M KOH electrolyte saturated with N2. (c) The values of Cdl of Co@CxNyC. (d) Chronoamperometric test for stability measurement of Co@C1N3C.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of (a) CQDs, and catalyst of (b) Co@CxNyC.

head.ce:title: In situ construction of N-doped hollow carbon nanotubes anchored Co nanoparticles for bifunctional ORR/OER electrocatalyst

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors wish it to be known that, in their opinions, Zhiwei Lu and Qianqian Xiong should be regarded as joint First Authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of efficient, inexpensive, and stable bifunctional non-noble metal catalysts for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is of practical significance for the large-scale application of zinc-air batteries. Here, a novel bifunctional Co-based nano-catalyst (Co@CxNyC) is fabricated by complexation-reduction and high-temperature pyrolysis method. Co nanoparticles were loaded in nitrogen-doped hollow irregular carbon nanotubes derived from natural fibers and carbon quantum dots. Remarkably, the unique hollow structure and the Co-Nx active site make the screened Co@C1N3C have efficient ORR&OER catalytic performance. Co@C1N3C with onset potential (0.90 V) and half-wave potential (0.84 V) exhibited an efficient 4e− transfer path and favorable stability and methanol tolerance. Moreover, the overpotential of Co@C1N3C needed to achieve 100 mA cm−2 is 625 mV, which is 31 mV better than that of RuO2. In conclusion, this work not only provides deep insights into the catalytic activity of Co@CxNyC nanocomposite as ORR&OER bifunctional electrocatalysts but also guides the growth of Co-based bifunctional catalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, the situation of the energy crisis has become more and more severe [1–3]. Oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) as a cathodic reaction for fuel cells and metal-air batteries, and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) as an anode reaction for the electrolysis of water have attracted strong attention [4–6]. However, the high activation energy slows the dynamics of OER and ORR, which need more energy expenditure to achieve the desired reaction rate [7]. For example, the power of OER is slow, because it requires a multistep proton coupling process involving four-electron transfer, with a high overpotential associated with the higher activation energy forming the OO bond [8,9]. Precious metal catalysts such as platinum (Pt), and oxides of ruthenium (Ru) are widely considered the best electrocatalysts for OER and ORR. Nevertheless their high price, low storage capacity, and low durability severely handicap their application in practice [10–12]. Therefore, to overcome these challenges, it is necessary to design versatile electrocatalysts with superior performance but low prices.

Electrocatalysts with metallic properties can provide better electronic state and electron transport capacity [13]. In recent years, the development of single-functional non-noble metal-based catalysts (NPMCs) has made significant progress [14,15]. The transition metals iron [16], cobalt [17], and nickel [18] are regarded as extremely promising elements with the ability of catalytic electrolysis of water oxidation, but nickel and iron-based catalyst activity is low [19]. Therefore, cobalt-based catalysts due to their low price, non-toxic, and high activity are considered an excellent choice. Its variable chemical valence properties prompted the synthesis of outstanding ORR, OER catalytic activity, and corrosion resistance [20–22]. However, most cobalt-based catalysts such as Co salts [23], cobalt-based alloys [24], Co borides [25], and supported Co catalysts [26] have moderate activity and poor conductivity. The solution to this issue is to load cobalt-based materials onto heteroatom-doped carbon materials. This has two benefits: first, it can greatly increase the catalyst's conductivity; second, the confinement effect of carbon materials can prevent metal agglomeration, and at the same time, the introduction of heteroatoms into the carbon skeleton can obtain a better electronic structure and expose more active sites [27]. For example, Fan et al. [28] synthesized an in situ-anchored Co nanoparticle on nitrogen-doped carbon to serve as a catalyst for bifunctional ORR and OER. Qin et al. [22] synthesized Co3O4-x loaded on nitrogen-doped graphene oxide as an effective ORR and OER catalyst.

In addition, recent studies have shown that the combination of biomass carbon and cobalt-based materials exhibits excellent catalytic performance on ORR and OER. As Lu et al. [8] reported an in situ heterojunction of Co/N/C based on high-temperature pyrolytic graded porous carbon. The synthesized Co2N0.67-BHPC was comparable to Pt/C and was seized of excellent OER activity. Among them, the element components of the mushroom itself and the catalysis of porphyrin cobalt promote the formation of the Co/N/C heterojunction on the carbon cloth. Due to its special element composition, large specific surface area, and excellent network structure, biomass-derived carbon can protect metal particles from agglomeration and enhance the structural stability of composite materials [29]. Therefore, combining metals with biomass-derived carbon is a promising strategy to improve the overall catalytic performance, not only to increase the electrical conductivity of the catalyst but also to facilitate the associated mass transfer.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a class of carbon nanomaterials with outstanding fluorescence properties composed of quasi-discrete spherical carbon nanoparticles, which is less than 10 nm [30–32]. Biomass-derived carbon quantum dots can be used not only as a carbon source but also as a tuning of carbon-based surface electronic structure [33]. There are reports combining CQDs with ultra-thin NiFe-LDH nanosheets as OER catalysts by co-precipitation-solvent thermal method [34], and monofunctional catalysts loaded metal quantum dots onto cross-linked CQDs materials. However, no bifunctional catalysts combining CQDs with another biomass and loading transition metal nanoparticles emerged.

In this study, microcrystalline cellulose (MCel) and CQDs derived from pitaya skin were used as carbon sources, and cyanamide as nitrogen sources. The carbon source was treated under high-temperature conditions by the self-polymerization characteristics of cyanamide and then introduced Co(NO3)2·6H2O as a Co source. Co@C1N3C nanocomposite was prepared by high-temperature pyrolysis with a superior activity-specific surface area and excellent stability test results. The Co@CxNyC nanocomposite is a good ORR&OER bifunctional catalyst. It has the same half-wave potential (0.84 V) as the baseline substance Pt/C and the stability and methanol tolerance are better than that of Pt/C. The overpotential of 100 mA cm−2 is 625 mV, which is 31 mV better than the overpotential of RuO2. The excellent regulatory effect of CQDs and the construction of biomass-derived carbon nanotubes may be widely applied in the design and development of advanced functional materials.

Co@CxNyC is a Co-N-C material, and its preparation process is shown in Scheme 1 . Considering its abundant heteroatoms and low cost, we chose pitaya skin as the raw material for preparing CQDs solution. The preparation process is shown in scheme 1a. Then MCel and CQDs are used as carbon sources and cyanamide is used as nitrogen sources in a similar way to the post-treatment method for preparing nitrogen-doped carbon materials. The self-polymerization property of cyanamide under high temperatures is utilized to process the carbon sources. Coral-like structures (CNC) samples with considerable specific surface area, low density, and high carrying capacity were obtained. Then introduce Co(NO3)3⋅6H2O as a Co source. During high-temperature pyrolysis, Co3+ was reduced to Co NPs, which are embedded in N-doped carbon nanotubes self-assembled by coral-like structural materials to form Co@CxNyC. The metal cobalt produced by Co(NO3)2⋅6H2O will be wrapped in open carbon nanotubes to form the Co NPs catalytic site at the top of the nanotube, which can significantly improve the stability and reaction kinetics of Co@CxNyC.

The morphology and structure of the composite nanomaterials were illustrated by SEM. Fig. 1 a exhibited that Co@C1C presents a morphology and structure similar to the three-dimensional coral network. Co@C1N3 (Fig. 1b) and Co@C1N3C (Fig. 1c) show a tubular structure similar to a dandelion shape of 20 nm in diameter, with many smaller cylindrical branches on the surface of the irregular column. The existence of these structures makes the composites have a large specific surface area. The tubular tip is a nearly circular particle wrapped by a carbon layer. However, it is obvious that the particles at the top of the Co@C1N3 have an enlarged structure and are larger than that of the Co@C1N3C. The robust combination between CQDs and metals may modulate the Co@C1N3C structural network, forming size-appropriate tubular structures. TEM images in Fig. 1d-e indicate that the Co@C1N3C material forms Co NPs with a diameter of about 50 nm, which is encapsulated in inconsistent carbon nanotubes with coral-like structures. The high conductivity of carbon nanotubes facilitates electron transport and its constraint effect effectively hampers the aggregation of Co NPs, which enables Co@C1N3C to have outstanding ORR and OER activities under alkaline conditions. Some missing lattices can be found in HRTEM, suggesting some lattice defect in Co@C1N3C. The analyzed lattice spacing is 0.211 nm, corresponding to the (111) crystal surface of Co NPs (PDF # 97–0671,069). The lattice spacing is 0.344 nm, corresponding to the (002) crystal surface of carbon. This may be due to the interaction of carbon with Co NPs under high-temperature pyrolysis, jointly driving the lattice growth and allowing their lattices to cross over each other. Fig. 1f displays the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) map of Co@C1N3C. Diffraction loops associated with Co (111) and C (002) were observed, respectively, which are in agreement with the HRTEM results. Fig. S1 shows the results of the EDS test for Co@C1N3C, it shows that the material is mainly made up of three elements: C, N, and Co. Both Co and C achieved a quality percentage above 40%. Fig. 1g-k shows the SEM mapping of Co@C1N3C and the coexisting C, N, and Co elements are equably and densely distributed in the skeleton. The above results also confirmed that Co NPs are encapsulated in coral-like nanotubes, which is in line with the TEM results.

Fig. S2 shows the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curve for Co@C1N3C. The weight loss of Co@C1N3 is about 2.50% at the temperature between 30 °C and 150 °C, which is probably caused by the gradual removal of physically absorbed water with increasing temperature. The approximate 31.60% loss at the temperature between 350 °C and 900 °C could be due to the carbon backbone cleavage. Overall, the material has excellent performance around 350 °C. Moreover, according to the results of BET, the specific surface area of Co@C1N3C and the corresponding pore characteristics were taken by the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm. Fig. 2 a shows the diagram of the BET pore size results of Co@C1N3C. The adsorption curve is a typical adsorption hysteresis phenomenon. The sample presented similar N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm models for type IV, which indicated the existence of mesoporous. From Fig. 2a illustration, the average pore size of these mesoporous is about 4 nm. This mesoporous structure not only contributes to electrolyte adsorption and ion permeation but can also provide abundant active sites. Interestingly, it effectively promotes the diffusion of O2 and further improves the catalytic mass transfer efficiency and catalytic activity, which is more favorable to the ORR reaction.

The prepared Co@C1N3C was analyzed qualitatively by the XRD spectroscopy method. As shown in Fig. 2b, peaks at 2θ = 44.3°, 51.6°, and 75.9° can be observed in Co@C1N3C. These match the three different crystal surfaces of Co (PDF # 97-067-1069), (111), (200), and (220), respectively. The peaks of Co are highly consistent with the standard curve, indicating good crystallinity. Probably due to the higher diffraction peak intensity in this material, Co has a less pronounced carbon diffraction peak at 2θ = 20.0°. The chemical component and the bond configuration of Co@C1N3C were measured by XPS (Fig. 2c). The C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, and Co 2p peaks of Co@C1N3C are at the binding energies of positions 284.80, 532.04, 399.12 and 780.08 eV, respectively. The C 1s curve of Co@C1N3C (Fig. 2d) can be deconvoluted to two peaks at 284.8 and 285.5 eV and correspond to the configuration of C-C and C-O/C-N bonds, respectively. Compared with Co@C1C (Fig. S3), Co@C1N3C has a higher C-O/C-N content, indicating that Co@C1N3C forms a C-N structure during pyrolysis. The electronic modification of carbon orbit facilitates the adsorption-desorption capacity of the surface to O2 and OOH* intermediates, which has a momentous impact on ORR and OER reactions. The Co 2p curve (Fig. 2e) is deconvoluted into four peaks. Next Co 2p3/2 (782.2 eV) and Co 2p1/2 (797.0 eV) are accompanied by two satellite peaks at 786.4 eV and 802.2 eV, which is typical of Co2+. This result is in accord with the SEM and EDS results. The presence of numerous unreduced and uniformly dispersed Co2+ on the surface of this material, while Co NPs are encapsulated in a carbon layer. The N 1s curve of Co@C1N3C (Fig. 2f) was deconvolved into four peaks at 399.00, 401.10, and 403.24 eV, which are attributed to the Co-Nx [17], graphite N and oxidized-N [35]. In addition, previous studies have shown that metal-N bonds not only enhance electron transfer capacity [27] but also that Co-Nx and graphite N are often catalytic centers for ORR and OER. The high content of Co-Nx and graphite N is conducive to rapid catalytic electrochemical reactions [17]. Moreover, the binding energy of Co2p3/2 of Co@C1N3C shows a slightly higher transition compared to Co@C1N3 and Co@C1C (Fig. S5), which revealed a strong interaction between Co, N, and C in Co@C1N3C which alters the surface electronic structure of Co atoms [28].

The catalytic activity of ORR was assessed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) in an alkaline solution containing saturated O2. Fig. 3 a shows the CV curves in a solution saturated with N2 or O2 at 0.1 M KOH. It can be found from Fig. 3a that Co@C1N3C appears a reduction peak potential of around 0.79 V and the intensity of the oxygen reduction peak is greater than that of Pt/C. To explore the function of nitrogen-doped and CQDs-doped on the performance, Co@C1C, Co@C1N3, and Co@C1N3C were prepared, respectively. Fig. 3b shows the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) diagrams of Co@C1N3C material and the contrast material. Their onset potentials are 0.84 V, 0.87 V, and 0.90 V, respectively. Co@C1N3C has a similar half-wave potential of 0.83 V as Pt/C, and the onset potential is only 0.05 V different from Pt/C (onset potential = 0.95 V). After the addition of nitrogen source and CQDs, the onset potential and half-wave potential of the material are increased, which may be due to the adjustment of the position of cobalt nanoparticles in the nanotubes, exposing more active sites [36]. These results indicate that binding CQDs with heteroatom N to carbon nanotubes significantly enhances ORR activity. Moreover, near-circular Co NPs wrapped by a carbon layer and designed dandelion-like carbon nanotubes provide easier catalytic sites for the reaction.

RDE (0.196 cm2) tests were performed at 225–2025 r/min and RRDE (0.247 cm2) tests at 1600 r/min to verify the electrocatalytic kinetics of Co@C1N3C. The results of Fig. 3c show that the limiting current density increases uniformly with the increase of rotational speed in the range of 225–2025 r/min. The relevant Koutecky-Levich (K-L) plot (Fig. 3d) has good linearity at 0.2–0.6V. Based on the K-L equation, the average electron transfer number (n) is 3.8. The hydrogen peroxide yield (H2O2%) and RRDE-based electron transfer value (n) are shown in Fig. S6 and Fig. S7 respectively. The H2O2% of Co@C1N3C remained near 7.5%, which is comparable to the H2O2% of Pt/C. The n values of Co@C1N3C and reference substance Pt/C are between 3.84 and 3.96, 3.94 and 3.98 at the range of 0.2V and 0.8V, respectively. These results show that the material is involved in the four-electron transfer process toward ORR. In summary, the results of electrochemical tests indicate that Co@C1N3C has excellent ORR catalytic activity and a preferable kinetic process to Pt/C.

The durability and resistance to methanol are critical factors to measure electrocatalytic properties. The methanol resistance and durability of Co@C1N3C and Pt/C were further investigated and compared by comparing LSV curves and chronoamperometry tests. After methanol was added, there was a negligible current drop in the polarization curve of Co@C1N3C (Fig. 3e), indicating that it had good methanol resistance. However, after the addition of methanol, the polarization curve of Pt/C changed significantly (Fig. S10). Specifically, the methanol oxidation peak appeared after the addition of methanol. It was in line with the situation that methanol oxidation poisoned the catalyst and affected the intermediate adsorption, resulting in current inhibition. Fig. 3f shows the stability test results of the chronoamperometry. After continuous operation for 36,000 s, the current retention rate of Co@C1N3C is about 96% of the initial activity, higher than that of Pt/C (63%), indicating that Co@C1N3C has outstanding stability. Therefore, Co@C1N3C with excellent methanol tolerance and stability can compensate for the disadvantages of the catalyst Pt/C used for the ORR reaction, and in this respect, Co@C1N3C has the potential to replace Pt/C catalysts in a wide range of clean energy conversion systems.

The OER catalytic performance of Co@C1N3C was characterized by LSV and Tafel slope in a 1.0 M potassium hydroxide-saturated N2. The LSV polarization curves are seen in Fig. 4 a. When the current density is 100 mA cm−2, the Co@C1N3C electrocatalyst exhibits a potential of 625 mV, which is 31 mV superior to commercial catalyst RuO2 (656 mV), and 44 mV better than that of the second-best material Co@C1N5C (669 mV). This can be achieved by the appropriate amount of nitrogen, favoring the electron distribution to regulate the adsorption/desorption of the intermediates. As can be seen in Fig. 4a, with an increase in nitrogen concentration, the catalytic activity of OER exhibited a trend of first increasing and then declining. Thus, it is clear that the nitrogen admixture ratio for optimal OER activity is 1:3. To evaluate the electrocatalytic kinetics, a Tafel analysis of the different catalysts was obtained from their LSV polarization profiles. Tafel test results (Fig. 4b) show that the Tafel slope of Co@C1N3C is 95.8 mV·dec−1, RuO2 is 85 mV·dec−1, and Co@C1N1C and Co@C1N5C are both higher than that of Co@C1N3C. The slope of Tafel without CQDs (100 mV·dec−1) and nitrogen source materials (127.9 mV·dec−1) is higher than that of Co@C1N3C (95.8 mV·dec−1). The catalytic characteristics improve as the Tafel slope value decreases with decreasing overpotential. The values suggest that the catalytic performance of Co@C1N3C is significantly better than RuO2 and other materials, and the presence of nitrogen sources and CQDs has an important effect on the OER performance of Co@C1N3.

To obtain the active surface area of Co@C1N3C, CV tests with different scan speeds within the same potential range were used to evaluate the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) of the material. As can be seen from Fig. 4c, the Cdl of Co@C1N3C is 1.6 times that of Co@C1N5C, 2.3 times that of Co@C1N3, 4.4 times that of Co@C1C and 21 times that of Co@C1N1C, respectively. Therefore, a suitable nitrogen doping ratio and the introduction of CQDs enable Co@C1N3C to have a higher active surface area, which is more conducive to the OER reaction. Besides, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured to evaluate the reaction kinetics. The associated Nyquist plots are shown in Fig. S12. It can be seen that the Co@C1N3C has a lower charge-transfer resistance (Rct) and indicates a higher electrical conductivity. This is also one of the reasons for the outstanding catalytic performance of Co@C1N3C. Durability is also a prominent data to test the performance of OER catalysts. The timing current test (i-t) is used to test the material's stability. Test i-t data Co@C1N3C at a voltage of 1.85 V (vs. RHE) and an initial current density of approximately 100 mA cm−2. After continuous catalysis for 10 h, the current density of Co@C1N3C only decreased by about 20%. During the accumulation and release of O2, the i-t curve showed a typical zigzag pattern (Fig. 4d). The above results indicate that Co@C1N3C has a favorable catalytic OER stability.

In summary, a mixture of Mcel, CQDs, and cyanamide was utilized as the precursor for simple high-temperature pyrolysis that yielded a series of N-doped hollow carbon nanotubes loading with Co NPs. The optimal bifunctional catalyst Co@C1N3C was then obtained by varying the mass ratio of Mcel and cyanamide. In an alkaline solution, Co@C1N3C demonstrates much superior ORR catalytic activity than Pt/C. Besides, the OER test findings indicate that the peak potential of Co@C1N3C is 625 mV at a current density of 100 mA cm−2, which is superior to that exhibited by commercial catalyst RuO2 (31 mV). Co@C1N3C's exceptional performance is a result of its special structure. The Co-wrapped N-doped hollow carbon nanotubes, which resemble bamboo, shield the Co-active sites while acting as a conduit for quick electron transport. However, the combination of N and Co, the high graphitic carbon content, and the increased number of active sites also improve their ORR&OER. This work offers a reference approach for the development of bifunctional ORR&OER catalysts loaded with N and Co. As well as a novel concept for the synthesis of carbon compounds using biomass materials as precursors. From what has been discussed above, we took a similar post-processing method for the preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon materials. Use MCel as a carbon source together with the CQDs. Use the cyanamide under the condition of high temperature since the polymerization characteristics deal with carbon source and then introduce Co(NO3)2⋅6H2O as Co source. Nitrogen-doped carbon nanocomposites containing Co NPs were prepared by high-temperature pyrolysis. The selected Co@C1N3C material has a dandelion-like morphology and structure, and Co NPs are encapsulated in hollow carbon nanotubes with irregular sizes. The results of the electrocatalytic ORR test show that the material has an onset potential and half-wave potential close to commercial Pt/C. It has a 4e− transfer path, good methanol resistance, and stability. The OER test findings indicate that the peak potential of Co@C1N3C is 625 mV at a current density of 100 mA cm−2, which is preferable to that exhibited by commercial catalyst RuO2 (31 mV). Its Tafel slope is the closest to that of RuO2. Among the series Co@CxNyC materials, it has the best Cdl value. The active specific surface area is large, and the stability test results are good. The results of physical characterization and electrochemical performance tests show that Co@CxNyC nanocomposites with ORR&OER bifunctional catalysis have been successfully synthesized, which has the potential to replace commercial Pt/C and RuO2.

Zhiwei Lu: Investigation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Qianqian Xiong: Investigation, Data curation, Conducted experiments, Validation, Writing – original draft. Rao Fu: Data curation, Conducted experiments. Wenli Wang: Methodology, Data curation. Lan Zhang: Investigation, Supervision, Data curation. Minglei Yan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Supervision, Formal analysis. Chun Wu: Investigation, Validation, Resources, Funding acquisition. Mengmeng Sun: Supervision, Project administration, Resources. Gehong Su: Supervision, Project administration, Resources, Funding acquisition. Yanying Wang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Funding acquisition. Jianshan Ye: Methodology, Supervision, Resources. Hanbing Rao: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 22104102, and 32371522), Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province (Grant Nos. 2023NSFSC0618, 2022NSFSC1266, and 2022NSFSC1924), Key Laboratory of Fuel Cell Technology of Guangdong Province (FC202205), and the Two-Way Support Team Programs of Sichuan Agricultural University (Grant No. P202105).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2024.02.283.
